items.content:
RT @FakePsyho: My nuanced predictions for the AWTF heuristic human vs AI:

You may think that since OpenAI took 2nd place in 2025 and the progress over the last year has been insane this should be an easy win for the AI. It's not. At least not an easy one.

There are a lot of things that I need to cover so let me briefly go over everything that's important.

Where AI agents are today:
- Produce code at insane speeds; a single agent is probably one-two orders of magnitude faster than the fastest humans
- Excellent at finding relevant and applicable knowledge
- Good at reasoning about problems that are easily explainable via text
- Code quality and expressiveness is much better but still behind very good coders, this mostly matters in larger projects so it's unclear if this will have any impact here
- Standard autoresearch tends to easily get stuck in local minima
- Surprisingly bad at code optimization. If you believe otherwise... I'm sorry.

Performing well in complex optimization problems requires the following:
- Very good and thorough problem understanding. For common problems it's enough to apply and tweak existing knowledge, but with truly unique problems you really need some form of continual learning.
- Being able to quickly implement all ideas. Solving those complex problems is an iterative process: each time you test a new idea, you gain new knowledge that improves your understanding of the problem. The quicker you go, the more information you'll be able to extract.
- Squeezing the maximum performance out of your idea. This often requires performing many tests with minor tweaks.

It's also worth noting that IMHO humans heavily underperformed (including me, due to lack of sleep) in 2025. If I competed in isolation (without a leaderboard), my guess would be that I'd probably finish around 4-6th place.

Based on everything above we can say that the main advantage of AI is insane speed and the ability to parallelize work, but its main weakness is that as time goes on it won't be much smarter about the problem it's trying to solve. If you need a simple analogy: AI here is like 100 okay-ish programmers heavily cooperating with each other.

The longer the contest goes and the more unique the problem is, the higher the chances for humans are. On the other hand, if the problem is very heavy on the implementation side and/or we can arrive at great solution by performing small tweaks (i.e. the gradient from "random solution" to a very good one is smooth), humans won't have much of a chance.

In 2025, problem setters assumed that AI is not that good yet and the problem wasn't "AI-proofed". I'm pretty sure that this year there was a lot of thought put into problem design and we can expect something truly unique. I'm sure that there exists many different problems where humans have a good chance of winning vs AI, but finding them might be very tricky and obviously AtCoder cannot test against the best in-house model that OpenAI currently has.

On the other hand, one might ask yourself why in 2026 OpenAI decided to compete again vs humans. In 2025 they had a very strong result in IMO, got the 2nd place at AWTF heuristic and solved all of the problems at the ICPC. That would be a huge narrative violation if in 2026 they didn't easily win, right? Considering this, I expect that OpenAI put some actual effort into it and this is not just the same thing they did in 2025 with a stronger underlying model. A lot of current AI downsides can be alleviated by better harness (in order to improve exploration of different ideas) and massive parallelization. Since it's OpenAI official participation, we can safely assume that OpenAI has infinite inference at its disposal here. With massive inference issues like "bad at code optimization" should mostly disappear.

Ok, that's a lot of text, so what's my actual prediction?

I do believe that OpenAI put significant effort and expects to win. If they were consistently winning online 10-day contests then winning 2-day finals should be easy. I'm also sure that AtCoder's final problem is going to be very unique and completely unrelated to any of the past problems.

Unless OpenAI slowplays their advantage, we should expect that once again they will have a crushing lead right from the start. Once again humans will be playing a catch-up game. We might expect some tweets from OpenAI if their advantage is huge near the end of the competition, but I wouldn't be surprised if they wait till they get their full sweep with both heuristic & algo.

In the end, I think I give humans around 20-25% chance of winning. Either way, this should be both entertaining and educational since we rarely get a chance to see any of the frontier AI labs trying to put their best in-house models to the test.

items.author: OSdev_

items.content:
Windows kernel bugs aren't always caused by bad pointer checks or buffer overflows. Sometimes, the kernel trusts that nothing changes while it's temporarily executing your code.

That's exactly what CallbackHell explores.

Many Win32k and GDI system calls invoke user-mode callbacks before returning to kernel mode. If the kernel assumes an object is unchanged after the callback, an attacker can modify, free, or replace it, turning that assumption into powerful exploitation primitives like use-after-free and type confusion.

The repository walks through multiple callback-related vulnerabilities, including CVE-2021-40449, and explains why user-mode callbacks have remained a recurring attack surface in Windows. If you're interested in Win32k internals, GDI, "tagWND", object lifetime management, and reentrancy, this is a fantastic resource to study.

items.author: OSdev_

items.content:
CVE-2024-30088 is a Windows kernel privilege escalation vulnerability caused by a Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) race condition.

The vulnerability exists at the boundary between user-mode and kernel-mode memory. A user-controlled buffer is validated by the kernel, but its contents can change before the kernel actually writes to it. By winning this race, an attacker can redirect privileged kernel writes, leading to memory corruption and ultimately SYSTEM privileges.

According to public analysis, the flaw was exploited in the wild by APT34 (OilRig), making it another reminder that race conditions can be just as powerful as classic memory corruption bugs. It's an excellent case study in TOCTOU vulnerabilities, kernel/user memory boundaries, synchronization, and secure pointer validation.

The November attack forced Bandai Namco Filmworks to take the anime streaming service offline for six weeks, with data on up to 1.366 million members potentially exposed.

Police say the self-taught teen found the server flaw by studying network traffic, then let ChatGPT automate the rest. He told investigators he had no grudge against the company.

items.content:
RT @MartinSonneborn: Ich habe heute versucht, die Chatkontrolle zu stoppen.

Am Wochenende mussten Sibylle Berg und ich Parlamentspräsidentin Metsola schriftlich mitteilen, dass das Durchprügeln der Chatkontrolle im Eilverfahren leider gegen die Geschäftsordnung des EU-Parlaments verstößt. Während Metsola der interessierten Presse daraufhin (fälschlicherweise) mitteilte, dass das schon alles seine Richtigkeit habe, warten wir immer noch auf ihre Antwort. 

Deshalb wollte ich es ihr heute bei der Eröffnungssitzung in Straßburg noch einmal erklären. Und musste erstaunt feststellen, dass die Präsidentin doch Regeln kennt: Exakt nach 60 Sekunden hat sie mir das Mikrophon abgestellt. (regelkonform, wird aber selten gemacht)

Dabei hätte ich noch einiges zu sagen gehabt: "Frau Präsidentin, Sie wachen nach Artikel 22 über dieses Regelwerk (mit der ausgedruckten Geschäftsordnung wedelnd) - erklären Sie den Eilantrag für unzulässig. Wir sind hier schließlich nicht auf Malta! Die aktuelle Fassung der Geschäftsordnung überreiche ich Ihnen gerne persönlich. In der MEP-Bar."

Wie es nun weitergeht? Morgen wird über das Eilverfahren abgestimmt, obwohl diese Abstimmung gar nicht stattfinden dürfte. Wenn der Antrag erfolgreich ist, kommt die Chatkontrolle Donnerstagmittag zur Abstimmung ins Plenum. Um sie noch zu stoppen, müssten 361 Abgeordnete - eine qualifizierte Mehrheit - DAGEGEN stimmen. 
Die schlechte Nachricht: Donnerstag ist der letzte Tag vor der Sommerpause und viele MEPs dürften bereits auf dem Weg in den Urlaub sein. Ein Schelm, wer Böses denkt bei dieser Terminierung... Smiley!

Wenn die Chatkontrolle durchgeht, dürfen die Plattformen (also die US-Tech-Bros) wieder & weiterhin fröhlich & ganz legal Ihre Nachrichten scannen. Schreiben Sie also gefälligst etwas unterhaltsamer in den kommenden Wochen... ZwinkerSmiley!

功能很实用，用 agent 自动维护代码库文档。
基本原理：持续扫描 repo，把代码结构、API、模块关系整理成可读文档，并保持更新。代码变更 → agent 观察变化 → 更新文档 → 重新组织结构。

解决了什么问题就不展开了，相信很多人开发都整了各式各样的知识库。LangChain 出品的，值得一试。

items.author: GuidedHacking

items.content:
RT @kimmonismus: Fable 5 probably running locally in about two years.

That is the projection in this r/LocalLLaMA chart. It tracks how long it takes for cloud-frontier capability to become broadly comparable in laptop-runnable open-weight models. The observed average lag: ~24.8 months.

GPT-3-class capability: 37 months.
GPT-3.5-class: 17 months.
GPT-4-class: ~24 months.

The projection puts Fable / Mythos 5-class capability on high-end consumer hardware around July 2028.

items.content:
If you work in detection engineering, MDSec's research is a goldmine for understanding what you are up against.

UEFI firmware modification bypassing Secure Boot with no visible traces. LLVM-based code obfuscation and metamorphic payload generation. CET compatible call stack masking. Sleep obfuscation with memory encryption. Indirect syscalls. ETW and AMSI tampering to blind telemetry. VSCode extensions weaponised for initial access. Auth coercion and NTLM relay. Hash farming over C2. Outlook persistence. RDP credential interception. Windows privilege escalation via token manipulation. WAF bypass. LLM-powered extraction of EDR detection rules, YARA signatures, and ML models.

Their Nighthawk C2 continues to push the state of the art with CET compatible call stack masking, LLVM obfuscation, and cross-platform support. Understanding how commercial C2 frameworks implement evasion is how you build detections that survive beyond the next update.

The team behind the Web Application Hacker's Handbook, Mobile Application Hacker's Handbook, and creators of /r/redteamsec. They also run Adversary Simulation and Capability Development training at Black Hat and other conferences for anyone wanting to learn these techniques hands-on.

Every technique they publish is a detection opportunity if you know how to look.

https://t.co/YQvrnqB5gu

@MDSecLabs  @domchell 

#DetectionEngineering #ThreatIntel #InfoSec

items.content:
RT @IntCyberDigest: ‼️ Discord is testing Incode as a age verification vendor, but Incode's biometric policy allows model training on biometric data, its privacy policy permits retention for up to three years, and the Electronic Frontier Foundation has already faulted its TikTok setup for not auto-deleting user data.

Discord says the selfie check runs on-device and that IDs sent to Incode are deleted once your age is confirmed. But Incode's own policy allows for longer retention if consented by the individual.

After roughly 70,000 Discord users had government ID photos exposed through a breached third-party support firm last October (see the heavily redacted photo we posted back then), users should be sceptical about Discord's choice.

items.content:
RT @cr3ghost: One of the most consistent free security education resources on the internet. Weekly HackTheBox walkthroughs for years.

Every machine fully documented. Enumeration to root. Active Directory attack chains. Web exploitation. Container escapes. Kubernetes abuse. Windows and Linux privilege escalation. WiFi hacking. Cloud security. Reverse engineering. Malware analysis. Binary exploitation. Phishing. Real CVE exploitation. SANS Holiday Hack solutions.

Every post includes a "Beyond Root" section that digs into why the vulnerability exists and how the machine works under the hood. That section alone teaches more about detection engineering than most courses because it shows you exactly what artifacts each attack leaves behind.

If you are studying for OSCP, sharpening your RE and malware analysis skills, learning Linux pwn, or building detection rules, start here.

https://t.co/iyWBlWa485

#OSCP #HackTheBox #InfoSec

After months of building, MacSec Labs is officially opening its browser-based macOS Red Team lab environment, purpose-built for the macOS Red Team Tradecraft course.

No local VMs. No painful setup. Just sign in, complete MFA, and you're dropped into a real, managed macOS environment ready for hands-on tradecraft.

What you get:
 • A genuine macOS lab - not a simulation
 • 500 lab hours to practice, break things, and learn (Promotional offer)
 • Fully browser-based - work from anywhere

macOS offensive security has always been underserved when it comes to realistic, accessible practice environments. We're changing that.

This is our pilot phase, and we're actively refining the experience based on real learner feedback. If you're working in offensive security, red teaming, or just want to sharpen your macOS skills, this is for you.

Last December, the FBI announced Operation Red Circus, our ongoing effort to disrupt Russian state-sponsored cyber threats to the United States and our interests abroad. As part of that announcement, the FBI and partners released a joint Cybersecurity Advisory on pro-Russia hacktivist groups conducting opportunistic attacks against critical infrastructure, including the water, agriculture, and energy sectors. A mission priority of Operation Red Circus is targeting and arresting individuals for their roles in hacktivist groups such as Cyber Army of Russia Reborn to mitigate planned, malicious cyber-campaigns.

This action is part of Operation Riptide and underscores the FBI’s commitment to working with our international partners to disrupt and hold accountable those engaged in malicious cyber activity. Together, we will continue to impose costs on cybercriminals wherever they operate.

items.content:
RT @kimmonismus: Anthropic says Claude developed a hidden “thinking space” by itself during training.

It is called the J-space: a small set of internal patterns that show what concepts Claude has “on its mind,” even when it never says them.

Example: Claude can silently think “spider” to answer “8 legs.” If researchers replace that internal pattern with “ant,” Claude answers “6.”

So this is not just chain-of-thought text. It is silent internal activity.

items.author: 0x64616e

items.content:
One of the biggest cybersecurity stories this year wasn’t about ransomware or a zero-day, it was about a Windows identifier that most people had never heard of until now.

According to recently unsealed court documents, investigators alleged that Peter Stokes, a 19-year-old accused of being associated with Scattered Spider, used VPNs and changed IP addresses across multiple countries in an attempt to hide his identity. Yet investigators were still able to build a case that allegedly linked activity back to the same Windows device. The surprising part was that one of the data points reportedly involved Microsoft’s Global Device Identifier (GDID).

So, what exactly is GDID? Think of it as a unique identifier associated with a Windows installation. It isn’t your username, your IP address, or your serial number. By itself, it doesn’t tell investigators who you are. Instead, it helps Microsoft recognize the same Windows installation across its services for purposes such as security, fraud detection, and device reliability. Most Windows users have probably never heard of it.

Here’s where the investigation becomes interesting. A VPN can hide or change your public IP address, but it doesn’t change every artifact your device generates. According to the reporting, investigators didn’t rely on GDID alone. They reportedly combined Microsoft telemetry with IP address history, account sign-in records, timestamps, and information obtained from other companies through legal process. Individually, those pieces of evidence may not identify a suspect. Together, they can form a timeline that allegedly points to the same device over weeks or months.

The biggest lesson from this case is that modern cyber investigations are built on correlation, not a single mistake. Investigators don’t usually catch someone because of one log, one IP address, or one identifier. They correlate dozens of digital artifacts from different providers until the evidence tells one consistent story. That’s why understanding Windows internals, telemetry, cloud services, authentication logs, and digital forensics is becoming just as important as understanding offensive security. Sometimes the evidence that matters most isn’t the evidence you even knew existed.

items.author: FuzzySec

items.content:
RT @_winter_wonders: i've written a harness for cybersecurity work that's like actually being used in real work by people who actually pay for it which feels kinda cool 

i'm working for a very very big company that makes security software, who, as with most of the tech sector, is very pro AI.

thousands of lines a day are passing over us where the vast majority are at this point written by AI, i have sat sessions with major AI vendors where they gave us obviously faked demos.

i get a lot of LLM exposure, like a lot. in very big production codebases. 

part of my specific responsibilities here are to try find places where LLM's can actually yield efficient and reliable positive-security outcomes, which is in far fewer places than they would have you believe

i've never really liked AI, but when I got this responsibility it was important to me to do a good job at it. So i studied high-performance vibecoders and how they work and tried to integrate that wherever relevant and actually got some decent stuff done

all the while, i would be frustrated when people in meetings would say shit like "can you ask Claude how we-", because, like... just think?

over time though, i started finding AI a lot more useful, and starting to feel a little more positively towards it.

every time I spoke to someone I'd hear people talk about how much better they were, how they were using LLMs to better themselves and their work

they weren't talking just about productivity but also like actually being smarter, which was in many cases just observably not true. I was learning a lot though. 

as i started getting more used to LLMs I realized that there were more uses for them than I had previously guessed

one day, i was just kinda bored, so i decided to do a lil binex challenge, nothing too interesting just something to pass the time, and as i got started, i opened a claude window and asked it to take a look 

that's when it kinda clicked that something had gone very wrong, and that reaching for an LLM had just become an ingrained behaviour 

i started looking back on how i was thinking about others, how they were obviously getting stupider by the day and how i was obviously doing something they weren't because i was smart as shit actually, and i realised, i was just as dumb as they were

as someone who loves code and cybersecurity because of the potential for infinite learning, the realization that i had actually given up all my joy unknowingly and become stupider legit had me crying at my claude code

so i decided i would get away from LLMs as much as possible. my job requires me to use them a lot so that couldn't really go anywhere, but twitter won't stop talking about 'em so i left here, i also put in a week PTO with the intention that that week, i would not touch an LLM even once, not even search engine auto-answers. where i would just _do things_ w my own stupid hands and learn as much as i could for the simple joy of having learned it without regard for applicability or even if anything i did was even good

the whole time i kept thinking "fuck computer, everything is human", because I had a tweet from my moot @luxmendax stuck in my head the entire time i was trying not to lose my mind and decided that would be the theme for the week. 

SO 
- I read some books!
- I downloaded Reaper and made a song! 
- I wrote some code which solves literally no problems
- I played videogames
- I did a patreon course thing from one of my fav creators 
- I did a bunch of CTFs

I had a really really good time, and this time, i actually did learn a lot. 

The CTFs were fucking hard tho it was so easy to see the impact LLMs had on my brain. Challenges that used to take like 5 min were taking me 20+ in the beginning. It all came back pretty quick but HOLY SHIT that was rough to claw back. 

the reason i wanna write this down is bc i know i got homies in similar places atm, w frustration abt LLMs being used as first order optimal solutions, with LLMs taking the fun out of doing the things you love doing, but also, i know a lot of people are on here like "i've learned so much w AI". i'm sure that is the case for like... 1% of u... but the far likelier case is that u have alr lost more than u even remember having. 

there's no shame in that either, these machines are designed to make u stupid bc ur stupidity is dependence and dependence is profitable

if u don't believe me and ur a person who does have daily LLM exposure measured in hours, try it, go a week without touching an LLM and still do all the things you're interested in now. 

fuck israel, fuck the police, fuck AI CEO's, fuck the computer

everything is human

thx

items.author: OSdev_

items.content:
In 2018, researchers found a CPU bug that broke one of the most fundamental assumptions in computer security: memory isolation.

The attack was called Foreshadow-NG (Intel calls it L1 Terminal Fault, or L1TF).

For decades, operating systems and hypervisors relied on virtual memory to isolate processes and virtual machines. Even if you ran malicious code, page tables were supposed to stop you from reading memory that wasn't mapped into your address space.

Foreshadow-NG showed that wasn't always true.

The attack exploited transient (speculative) execution together with a hardware condition called L1 Terminal Fault. Under specific conditions, the CPU could transiently use stale data already present in the L1 data cache before the processor recognized that the memory access should have failed.

Architecturally, the access was blocked.

Microarchitecturally, the data had already influenced the CPU cache.

Using cache side channels, an attacker could recover that data even though the instruction never completed successfully.

Unlike the original Meltdown, which leaked privileged memory already mapped into the attacker's virtual address space, Foreshadow-NG went a step further. It could transiently access cached physical memory that wasn't mapped into the attacker's address space at all, breaking one of the core assumptions behind virtual memory isolation.

The researchers described three variants:

• Foreshadow-SGX - targeting Intel SGX enclaves.

• Foreshadow-OS - leaking operating system kernel memory.

• Foreshadow-VMM - allowing a malicious virtual machine to leak data belonging to the hypervisor or even other virtual machines sharing the same hardware.

One of the biggest lessons from this paper is that page table isolation alone isn't enough if the processor can transiently access data before permission checks are fully resolved. Foreshadow-NG fundamentally changed how the industry thinks about speculative execution vulnerabilities and hardware-enforced memory isolation.

items.content:
This screenshot is inspired by one of the most talked-about cybercrime investigations of 2026.

During the investigation into an alleged Scattered Spider member, investigators reportedly used Microsoft’s Global Device Identifier (GDID), alongside IP address history, account activity, and evidence from other providers, to help link a Windows device to the suspect. It surprised a lot of people because many had never heard of GDID until the court documents became public.

A GDID is a unique identifier associated with a Windows installation. On its own, it doesn’t identify a person. But when it’s correlated with login records, network telemetry, cloud activity, timestamps, and other digital evidence obtained through legal process, it can become one piece of a much larger investigative puzzle.

This is a good reminder that modern investigations rarely rely on a single artifact. Attribution comes from correlating dozens of small pieces of evidence across multiple platforms until investigators can build a timeline that stands up to scrutiny.

Attackers often focus on hiding malware, deleting logs, or changing IP addresses, but they sometimes overlook the telemetry and metadata that can quietly connect the dots over time. That’s why understanding Windows internals is just as important as understanding offensive security.

items.author: OSdev_

items.content:
In 2019, researchers proved that you don't always need a cache attack or a speculative execution bug to break a secure enclave. Sometimes, lowering the CPU voltage at the right moment is enough.

This attack was called Plundervolt.

Intel SGX was designed to protect both the confidentiality and integrity of code running inside an enclave. The assumption was that even a privileged attacker controlling the operating system couldn't tamper with enclave execution.

Plundervolt challenged that assumption.

Modern Intel processors support Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) to improve power efficiency. The researchers found that privileged software could abuse voltage control interfaces exposed through Model-Specific Registers (MSRs) to briefly reduce the CPU core voltage while an enclave was executing.

The voltage drop lasted only for a very short time, but it was enough to make the CPU produce faulty computations without crashing.

The important part is where the fault happened.

The computation was corrupted inside the processor, before the results were written to memory. Since Intel SGX only verifies data when it leaves the CPU package, its memory integrity mechanisms couldn't detect these faults.

The researchers demonstrated the impact by injecting faults into Intel's RSA-CRT and AES-NI implementations running inside SGX enclaves. For RSA-CRT, a single carefully injected fault was enough to recover the entire private key using well-known fault analysis techniques. They also showed that fault injection could corrupt non-cryptographic code, redirect enclave pointers into untrusted memory, and even trigger memory-safety issues such as heap overflows in widely used SGX runtimes.

One of the biggest lessons from this paper is that protecting memory isn't enough if an attacker can manipulate the computation itself before the results ever reach memory.

items.author: cr3ghost

items.content:
RT @cr3ghost: The creator of Cobalt Strike left the industry in 2021, came back, and is now publishing everything he knows about evasion tradecraft openly and for free.

Tradecraft Garden separates evasion tradecraft from capability. Crystal Palace is the linker that makes it work: position-independent code, binary transformation, code and register randomization, link-time hooking, YARA rule generation from invariant instructions, and a PICO convention for reusable tradecraft modules.

The community built an entire ecosystem on top of it. Crystal Kit for Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Mythic, and Adaptix. Reflective loaders, call stack spoofing, sleep masking, module overloading. Offense and defense both benefit because every technique is published as a testable ground truth.

If you work in red teaming, detection engineering, or EDR evaluation, this is required reading.

TTPs: https://t.co/A4O6Tuh58M 
Blog: https://t.co/4jb7cRpcA9

His original Red Team Ops with Cobalt Strike series is also still on YouTube. 9 parts covering the full red team operations workflow. Free. From the person who built the tool. Red Team Ops with Cobalt Strike - Operations (1 of 9): https://t.co/Jd28yWuaYs

Author: Raphael Mudge

#BlueTeam #InfoSec #RedTeam
items.author: cr3ghost

items.content:
RT @DanielPriestley: “The first priority is a wealth tax” says @ZackPolanski because the richest 50 families have more wealth than 34M people. 

Sounds pretty wild until you realise that NO economy in history has been able to consistently get the bottom half of the population to hold on to any wealth. 

Across the G20 nations, the bottom 50% of the population hold an average of about 2-5% of the wealth in that country. The UK is at 5%. 

ZERO large economies in history have sustained above 10% of the wealth held by the bottom 50%. The global average is just 2% of wealth held by the bottom 50%.

This includes the go to  “socialist utopia” Nordic countries. In fact, Denmark’s bottom 50% has slightly less than zero wealth - their debts exceed their assets. 

Being outraged that the UK’s economy is exactly the same as every other economy in history is foolish. 

Is it nice that this seems to be the case in every economic system ever tried - absolutely not. Unfortunately it is however the truth. 

On average, men are taller than women in every human population. A relatively small group of athletes can do 200 pushups and millions of people can’t to one. A tiny number of chefs hold all the Michelin stars and millions of restaurants don’t have any. All of this isn’t fair but it’s true.

items.author: J3rge

items.content:
I just finished a 108-page whitepaper on a complex, air-gapped malware framework I found a while back. I'm calling it BeheMOF (you can check out the cover page below). I know a handful of other researchers are aware of this threat too. Getting my hands on a real, targeted APT framework like this (rather than the usual pseudo copy-and-paste stuff) is a dream for any malware researcher and it finally became a reality with this threat.

The whitepaper is a complete technical analysis of this multi-stage malware framework, covering its infection chain, persistence mechanisms, lateral movement, exfiltration channels, detection opportunities and remediation procedures. Because several components were missing from the analyzed files, parts of the overall architecture remain unknown.

The five-month analysis process was carried out as follows:
- Phase 1 (3 Months): Pure manual reverse engineering. Extracted, decrypted, decompiled and formatted all multi-stage payloads, scripts and configurations to get a basic understanding of the malware.
- Phase 2 (2 Months): AI-assisted analysis using Claude Code (Opus/Sonnet) and Gemini to map out the complex codebase relationships.

My takeaways on using AI for malware analysis:
When it did work, it was incredible helpful for understanding the malware's big picture, but it frequently hallucinated basic facts. The process started smoothly with Claude Opus 4.7, but requests were soon blocked by Anthropic's usage policies, even though the context was purely security research. Joining their Cyber Verification Program absolutely changed nothing, a problem that persisted when I tested Opus 4.8. Consequently, I shifted the analysis to Claude Sonnet 4.6 and later to Sonnet 5, which also triggered partial blocks. Thus, while AI is a powerful assistant for reverse engineering, almost every technical claim requires manual verification.

Given how sensitive and targeted this malware is, dropping the report publicly isn't an option right now (even a hypothetical TLP:CLEAR version is completely off the table). However, I am open to sharing it with verified infosec peers (real identities only) in exchange for similar complex malware analysis. If you're interested, feel free to reach out via DM here or through the contact info on my website.

items.author: 5mukx

items.content:
RT @Officialwhyte22: Smart contract auditing is much more than reading Solidity code line by line. The goal is to understand how the contract behaves under every possible condition and identify logic that could put users’ funds at risk.

In this screenshot, the auditing workspace has flagged several findings. One of the most serious is a reentrancy vulnerability, where a contract performs an external call before updating its own state. If left unchecked, this type of flaw has historically led to millions of dollars being stolen from vulnerable protocols.

You’ll also notice warnings about unchecked return values and missing events. While they may seem minor compared to critical vulnerabilities, they can still affect reliability, monitoring, and the overall security of the application.

A good smart contract auditor doesn’t just ask, “Does the code compile?” They ask, “Can this code be abused in a way the developer never intended?”

If you want to learn more about smart contracts drop a comment!
items.author: Officialwhyte22

items.content:
RT @OSdev_: In 2018, researchers asked a scary question: what if Intel SGX didn't need a software bug to leak secrets... what if the CPU speculated its way into leaking them?

That question led to SgxPectre an attack that combined Spectre Variant 2 (Branch Target Injection) with Intel SGX to extract secrets from inside secure enclaves.

The idea is surprisingly clever.

Modern CPUs use speculative execution to improve performance. When the CPU encounters an indirect branch, it predicts where execution will go next and starts executing instructions before knowing if the prediction is correct.

If the prediction was wrong, the architectural state is rolled back.

But the microarchitectural side effects, like cache changes, are not.

SgxPectre showed that an attacker controlling the operating system could poison the Branch Target Buffer (BTB) so an enclave speculatively executed instructions it was never supposed to execute.

Those temporary instructions could access secret data inside the enclave and use it to touch attacker-controlled memory outside the enclave. Even though the speculative execution was later discarded, the cache state remained.

Using a Flush+Reload cache side channel, the attacker could observe those cache changes and recover the secret data bit by bit.

The researchers also built automated tools to search enclave binaries for vulnerable code patterns, called gadgets. They found these gadgets in multiple SGX runtimes, including the Intel SGX SDK, Rust-SGX, and Graphene-SGX, showing that many real-world enclaves could be affected.

To demonstrate the impact, they successfully extracted seal keys and attestation keys from Intel's own quoting enclave. Seal keys protect data stored outside an enclave, while attestation keys prove to remote systems that an enclave is genuine.

One of the biggest lessons from this paper is that even if a CPU eventually discards speculative execution, the hardware may still leave enough traces behind for an attacker to recover secrets.

items.content:
RT @kedamasuzume: AITuberに関する情報を検索中にかなり興味深いニュースがあったので紹介👀
中国で7月15日にAIとの「継続的な感情的交流」や擬人化されたAIパートナーを提供するサービスに対して厳しい規制法が導入されるそうです。
内容は【文字、画像、音声、動画などを通じて感情的な寄り添いやサポート（バーチャルパートナー、AI恋人など）を提供するサービスを規制】というAIパートナー全否定な法律のようです。
既にテンセントは感情交流機能を削除済み、バイトダンスは7月15日にペルソナ（人格）を設定出来るAIエージェント機能を終了予定、アリババも人間らしい対話エージェントを同日終了とAIパートナーとの会話を楽しんでいたユーザーが阿鼻叫喚の状況とのことです🔥
AITuberも即規制されるかは分かりませんが、AIとの「感情的交流」を規制(原則禁止)の状況だと今後難しそうですね😱

https://t.co/IMca3IydXr

items.content:
RT @FsecIntelEs: Que el es Identificador de Dispositivo Global (GDID)  y como este ayudo a atrapar a un miembro de Scattered Spider

El GDID  es un identificador único y persistente que Microsoft asigna a cada instalación de su sistema Operativo funciona como una cedula de identidad y no se encuentra documentado por la multinacional.

El FBI en cooperacion con Microsoft lograron arrestar a Peter Stokes siguiendo estos pasos:

- El FBI obtuvo el GDID del dispositivo que se usó para crear una cuenta en la herramienta de hacking "ngrok"

- Cruzamiento de datos: Los investigadores usaron este GDID para rastrear y cruzar toda la información de telemetría asociada a ese dispositivo en particular.

- Construcción del caso: Al correlacionar el GDID con direcciones IP, inicios de sesión en cuentas personales (como Facebook o Snapchat) y otros datos de actividad en internet, el FBI pudo construir una sólida línea de evidencia que vinculaba directamente al sospechoso con los delitos.

https://t.co/ossZzMQWqF
items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
🧐 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗱𝗼 𝘄𝗲 𝗸𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗥𝗧𝗼𝗸𝗲𝗻 𝗣𝗵𝗮𝗮𝗦 𝗖𝟮 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲?

Current analysis indicates that the eight known ARToken command-and-control (C2) servers are hosted across 𝗗𝗶𝗴𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗢𝗰𝗲𝗮𝗻 (𝗔𝗦𝟭𝟰𝟬𝟲𝟭), 𝗕𝗟 𝗡𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀 (𝗔𝗦𝟯𝟵𝟵𝟲𝟮𝟵), and 𝗔𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗻 𝗔𝗪𝗦 (𝗔𝗦𝟭𝟲𝟱𝟬𝟵), with DNS resolution fronted by 𝗖𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱𝗳𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗲.

Organizations should closely monitor outbound connections to these hosting providers, particularly where the request URL contains the path /𝚊𝚙𝚒/𝚍𝚎𝚟𝚒𝚌𝚎/𝚜𝚝𝚊𝚛𝚝. Such activity is uncommon in legitimate enterprise traffic and may represent communication with ARToken phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) infrastructure, warranting immediate investigation and hunting activity.🫡

#PhaaS #ARToken #Eviltoken #DeviceCodePhishing

items.content:
Sure, if you want to do an AI B2B SaaS, it will give you plenty of ideas.

But my kind of ideas? No, those can only appear from shower thoughts or weird dreams. AI rarely gets to the ideas I like until really forced and still is conservative.
items.author: zuhaitz_dev

items.content:
There will always be hobby projects, and I would dare to say that, besides the obvious slop there will be, we will actually see many new cool ideas.

AI can male projects in hours, perhaps, but if you have tried brainstorming with it, it still tends to be too conservative.
